schools.summary: The heaped-up terms of endearment: 'my brethren dearly beloved and longed for (epipothetoi — yearned after), my joy and crown' — the Philippians are Paul's joy now and his 'crown' (stephanos — the victor's wreath, his reward and boast) at the day of Christ; converts being the minister's joy and crown, his glory and reward; and the charge, drawing the conclusion ('therefore') from the heavenly hope of ch. 3, to 'stand fast (stand firm, hold your ground) in the Lord' — steadfast in faith and in union with Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: the Philippians are Paul's crown, the fruit of his labour that he would present to Christ; such love and such a reward are the ground of his urgent call that they stand fast in the Lord.

themes: my brethren dearly beloved and longed for, my joy and crown
so stand fast in the Lord, my dearly beloved
Paul's tender call to stand fast
the Philippians his joy and crown

schools.summary: Paul names two women of the church: 'I beseech (entreat) Euodias, and beseech Syntyche, that they be of the same mind (think the same thing, agree) in the Lord' — evidently two prominent members who had fallen into some disagreement; his even-handed appeal naming each separately, pleading equally with both to be reconciled and united 'in the Lord,' on the common ground of their faith, not taking sides or rebuking harshly (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: I beseech Euodias, and beseech Syntyche, that they be of the same mind in the Lord
Paul's plea to two women to be of one mind
an even-handed appeal for reconciliation
unity in the Lord

schools.summary: Paul asks a third party — 'true yokefellow (gnesie syzyge — genuine companion in labour; the identity uncertain, some taking syzygos as a proper name)' — to help reconcile the two women; these women had 'laboured with me (synethlesan — striven side by side) in the gospel,' Paul's fellow-workers, their disagreement the more to be healed for their past service; 'with Clement also, and with other my fellowlabourers, whose names are in the book of life' (the register of the saved), Paul commending them, assured their names are written in heaven (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: I intreat thee also, true yokefellow, help those women which laboured with me in the gospel
with Clement also... whose names are in the book of life
Paul's request to help the women
fellow-labourers in the book of life

schools.summary: Paul repeats the keynote (3:1): 'rejoice in the Lord alway (at all times, in every circumstance)' — Christian joy is to be constant, not dependent on changing conditions, for its source is the unchanging Lord; 'and again I say, Rejoice' — repeated for emphasis (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Cyril, Calvin): the joy is 'in the Lord,' grounded in Christ who is always the same, so it can be 'alway,' even in suffering and want; so important and contrary to nature in affliction is this duty that Paul doubles the command, that they may not miss it.

themes: Rejoice in the Lord alway: and again I say, Rejoice
the repeated call to rejoice in the Lord always
constant joy grounded in the unchanging Lord
the command doubled for emphasis

schools.summary: The charge: 'let your moderation (epieikes — gentleness, forbearance, reasonableness, the yielding, considerate spirit that does not insist on its own rights) be known unto all men' — believers are to be marked by a gentle, forbearing temper, evident to all, including patience under wrong, mildness, fairness, the opposite of a harsh, contentious, self-assertive spirit, shown even to opponents; 'the Lord is at hand (near)' — whether in his constant presence or his soon return, the motive for gentleness, knowing the Lord is near to help and to judge (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: Let your moderation be known unto all men
The Lord is at hand
the call to known gentleness
forbearance, for the Lord is near

schools.summary: The charge: 'be careful (merimnate — anxious, full of care, distracted with worry) for nothing' — believers are not to be consumed by anxiety about anything; the prescribed remedy being 'in every thing (in all circumstances) by prayer (general devotion) and supplication (specific petition) with thanksgiving' — every care turned into prayer, joined with gratitude for mercies already received; 'let your requests be made known unto God' — spreading their needs before God, casting their cares on him, the cure for anxiety (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): the burden carried to God in prayer is the cure for anxiety; believers are to spread every request before God with thanksgiving, and so find relief from care.

themes: Be careful for nothing; but in every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God
anxious for nothing but prayerful in everything
prayer with thanksgiving the remedy for worry
casting cares on God

schools.summary: The result of prayer: 'the peace of God (the tranquillity God gives, or the peace God himself possesses and imparts), which passeth (surpasses, transcends) all understanding (all human comprehension)' — a peace beyond what the mind can grasp or explain, either too great for the mind to comprehend or able to guard the soul better than all human reasoning can; 'shall keep (phrouresei — garrison, guard, as a military sentinel) your hearts and minds (thoughts) through Christ Jesus' — like a garrison protecting a city, God's peace stands sentinel over the believer's heart and thoughts (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: this peace, surpassing all human reason, garrisons the heart and mind in Christ; it is a tranquillity the world cannot give or understand, keeping the believer secure amid every trial.

themes: the peace of God, which passeth all understanding, shall keep your hearts and minds through Christ Jesus
the peace of God passing understanding
peace garrisoning the heart
secure through Christ

schools.summary: Paul's sweeping charge: 'whatsoever things are true (truthful, sincere), honest (semna — honourable, dignified, venerable), just (righteous, fair), pure (chaste, holy), lovely (prosphile — amiable, winsome), of good report (well-spoken-of, of fair repute)' — the whole compass of moral excellence; gathered up in 'if there be any virtue (moral excellence) and any praise (anything praiseworthy)'; 'think on (logizesthe — consider, ponder, take account of, let the mind dwell on) these things' — the believer to fill and govern his mind with what is good and noble (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: whatsoever things are true... honest... just... pure... lovely... of good report
if there be any virtue, and if there be any praise, think on these things
the call to think on whatsoever things are excellent
filling the mind with good

schools.summary: To right thinking (v.8) Paul adds right doing: 'those things, which ye have both learned (by instruction), and received (accepted as a tradition), and heard (from report), and seen in me (in my example), do (practise)' — let their conduct match what they were taught and saw in him; Paul appeals to his own teaching and example together as the pattern for their practice (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): to the peace of God (v.7) Paul adds 'the God of peace shall be with you' — the very God who gives peace will himself be present with those who so live; his presence is the reward and seal of obedience.

themes: which ye have both learned, and received, and heard, and seen in me, do
the God of peace shall be with you
doing what they learned of Paul
the presence of the God of peace

schools.summary: Paul turns to thank them for their gift: 'I rejoiced in the Lord greatly that now at the last your care (thoughtfulness) of me hath flourished again (anethalete — revived, blossomed afresh, like a plant in spring)' — their renewed care gladdened him; and his tactful care not to imply they had neglected him: 'wherein ye were also careful (mindful, concerned)' all along, 'but ye lacked opportunity' (no occasion or means presented itself to send) — their care had not failed, only the chance to express it (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry, Calvin): Paul rejoices in their love and tenderly excuses any seeming delay, attributing it not to coldness but to want of opportunity; his thankfulness is full of delicacy.

themes: I rejoiced in the Lord greatly, that now at the last your care of me hath flourished again
ye were also careful, but ye lacked opportunity
Paul's joy at the renewal of their care
tenderly excusing the delay

schools.summary: Paul guards his thanks from any appearance of complaint or begging: 'not that I speak in respect of want (not because I was in need, or am hinting for more)' — his joy was in their love, not in relief of his necessity; 'for I have learned (it was a thing learned, by experience and grace), in whatsoever state (circumstance) I am, therewith to be content (autarkes — self-sufficient, satisfied, independent of outward conditions)' — a contentment resting not on circumstances but on God (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): contentment is not natural but learned — a grace acquired through trials, by which the soul is satisfied in God whatever the outward lot.

themes: Not that I speak in respect of want
I have learned, in whatsoever state I am, therewith to be content
Paul's contentment in every state
contentment learned through grace

schools.summary: Paul's experience of both extremes: 'I know both how to be abased (brought low, in poverty and humiliation) and I know how to abound (have plenty)' — he had tasted both ends of the scale and learned how to bear each; 'every where and in all things I am instructed (memyemai — initiated, as into a secret or mystery) both to be full and to be hungry, both to abound and to suffer need' — initiated into the secret of contentment in every condition, neither lifted up by plenty nor cast down by want (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: I know both how to be abased, and I know how to abound
I am instructed both to be full and to be hungry, both to abound and to suffer need
Paul's learned mastery of plenty and want
initiated into the secret of contentment

schools.summary: Paul's confident declaration: 'I can do all things (I have strength for all things, I am equal to anything)' — not a boast of independent power, but of an ability that meets every demand; 'through Christ which strengtheneth me (endynamounti — pours strength into me, empowers me)' — the strength is not Paul's own but Christ's, infused into him; he can do all things only as Christ enables; the 'all things' here chiefly the bearing of all conditions (v.12), abasement and abundance, for which Christ gives sufficient strength (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the believer is equal to every circumstance through Christ, who supplies the strength; the all-sufficiency is not in self but wholly in the Lord who empowers.

themes: I can do all things through Christ which strengtheneth me
Paul's all-sufficiency through the strength of Christ
strength infused by Christ
equal to every condition in Christ

schools.summary: Having declared his contentment, Paul is careful not to seem to slight their gift: 'notwithstanding (yet) ye have well done (acted rightly, kindly) that ye did communicate with (synkoinonesantes — share, have fellowship in) my affliction' — their gift was a sharing in his trouble, making his affliction their own, an act of sympathy and partnership; though content and needing nothing, Paul gladly acknowledges that they did well (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Notwithstanding ye have well done, that ye did communicate with my affliction
Paul's commendation of their sharing in his affliction
fellowship in his trouble
content yet thankful for their gift

schools.summary: Paul recalls their early and unique generosity: 'in the beginning of the gospel (when it was first preached among them), when I departed from Macedonia, no church communicated with me (entered into partnership with me) as concerning giving and receiving, but ye only' — they alone supported him; the commercial figure 'giving and receiving' (as a ledger of accounts), the Philippians giving material support and Paul (and the gospel) giving spiritual blessing in return, a mutual partnership (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Cyril: of all the churches, only the Philippians had this fellowship with Paul in giving from the very first; their generosity was unique and steadfast, the mark of a peculiarly loving church.

themes: in the beginning of the gospel... no church communicated with me as concerning giving and receiving, but ye only
the Philippians alone supporting Paul from the first
giving and receiving, a mutual partnership
their unique generosity

schools.summary: Paul cites a specific proof of their early generosity: 'for even in Thessalonica (while he was there, soon after leaving Philippi) ye sent once and again (more than once, repeatedly) unto my necessity (to meet my need)' — their care reached him even there; 'once and again' meaning repeatedly, their giving not a single act but a recurring care, confirming the singular faithfulness just claimed (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

themes: For even in Thessalonica ye sent once and again unto my necessity
the Philippians' repeated gifts at Thessalonica
once and again, a recurring care
proof of their early faithfulness

schools.summary: Paul again guards his motive: 'not because I desire a gift (it is not the present itself I am after, nor do I value their love for what I receive)' — he is not seeking their money; 'but I desire fruit (the spiritual benefit, the reward of their giving) that may abound (increase, accumulate) to your account (to your credit, as in a ledger)' — Paul rejoices in their gift chiefly because of the spiritual fruit and reward it brings to them, caring more for their profit than his own relief (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Not because I desire a gift: but I desire fruit that may abound to your account
Paul desiring not the gift but their fruit
reward laid up to their account
caring for their profit, not his relief

schools.summary: Paul acknowledges full satisfaction: 'I have all (received everything, my receipt is in full) and abound (more than enough); I am full (completely supplied), having received of Epaphroditus the things which were sent from you' — their gift, brought by Epaphroditus, met his need abundantly; and the sacrificial figure (drawn from the Levitical offerings): their gift is 'an odour of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God' — their charity to Paul was, in effect, an offering presented to God himself, fragrant and acceptable to him (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): a gift to God's servant, given in love, rises to God as a sweet-smelling sacrifice; their kindness was a true act of worship, accepted and pleasing to God.

themes: having received of Epaphroditus the things which were sent from you, an odour of a sweet smell
a sacrifice acceptable, wellpleasing to God
their gift a sacrifice acceptable to God
their charity an offering of worship

schools.summary: Paul's confident promise in return for their generosity: 'my God (the God I serve and know) shall supply (fill up, fully meet) all your need (every want, spiritual and temporal)' — as they had supplied his need, God would supply theirs; 'according to his riches in glory (the measure of his own infinite, glorious wealth, not merely out of it but in proportion to it)' — God supplies not sparingly but according to the immeasurable riches of his glory; 'by Christ Jesus' — the supply reaching them through Christ, the channel of every blessing (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: But my God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus
the promise that God will supply all their need
supply according to his riches in glory
through Christ Jesus

schools.summary: The thought of God's glorious riches (v.19) overflows into a doxology: 'now unto God and our Father (God, who is our Father) be glory (praise, honour, the ascription of all glory) for ever and ever (unto the ages of the ages)' — Paul ascribes everlasting, unending glory to God; 'Amen' sealing it, the heart's assent and confirmation of the praise (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: Now unto God and our Father be glory for ever and ever. Amen
the doxology of glory to God forever
everlasting glory ascribed to God
sealed with Amen

schools.summary: Paul's closing greetings: 'salute (greet) every saint in Christ Jesus' — he sends his greeting to each individual believer, 'every saint,' omitting none, his love embracing all (not the leaders only but all the holy ones in Christ); 'the brethren which are with me (Paul's companions at Rome) greet you' — those with him join in the greeting, a token of the fellowship binding the churches (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Salute every saint in Christ Jesus
The brethren which are with me greet you
the greeting to every saint
from the brethren with Paul

schools.summary: Beyond his immediate companions, 'all the saints (all the believers at Rome) salute you' — the whole Roman church sends its greeting; 'chiefly (especially) they that are of Caesar's household' — there were believers even among the servants and officials of the imperial palace (Nero's household); the gospel had penetrated to the very court of Caesar, a token of its power and confirming (with 1:13) that the bonds reached the palace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: All the saints salute you, chiefly they that are of Caesar's household
the greeting from all the saints
believers in Caesar's household
the gospel reaching the imperial court

schools.summary: Paul closes, as he began (1:2), with grace: 'the grace (free favour, the whole blessing) of our Lord Jesus Christ' — the benediction that seals the letter; 'be with you all' — the comprehensive blessing embracing the whole church, every member without exception, grace for all; 'Amen' the seal, the heart's confirmation (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).
Cyril: the closing 'Amen' commits the church to the grace of Christ, the one source of all blessing, with which alone the believer can stand and rejoice.

themes: The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen
the closing benediction of grace
grace for the whole church
the seal of the epistle of joy
